}

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Default Description file_type string Yes — md, txt, or html frequency_threshold integer Yes — Min occurrences for inclusion minimum_token_count integer Yes — Min tokens per sentence scope string Yes — line, sentence, or block ngram_min integer No 2 Minimum n-gram size ngram_max integer No 5 Maximum n-gram size pos_filtering boolean No false Enable POS-tag filtering allowed_pos_tags array No [] Permitted Penn Treebank POS tags block_elements array No [] Elements for block scoping

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
pattern_extraction:
  file_type: md
  frequency_threshold: 3
  minimum_token_count: 4
  scope: sentence
  pos_filtering: false
  allowed_pos_tags: []
  block_elements: []
  ngram_min: 2
  ngram_max: 6

docs.title: Docker & Weaviate Integration
docs.text: Weaviate is an optional vector-database backend that enables semantic similarity search over your pattern library. It is not required for the core extraction, enrichment, clustering, or graph-export workflows.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Docker installed and running
  • An OpenAI API key (for the text2vec-openai module)

docs.title: 1. Set Your OpenAI API Key
docs.text:
export OPENAI_APIKEY=your-key-here

Add this to your shell profile (~/.zshrc, ~/.bash_profile) to persist it across sessions.

docs.title: 2. Start Weaviate
docs.text:
From the project root directory:

docker compose up -d

This launches Weaviate on port 8080 with a persistent weaviate_data volume.

docs.title: 3. Verify Weaviate is Running
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8080/v1/.well-known/ready
# Expected: {"status":"READY"}

docs.title: Maintenance Commands
docs.text: Action Command Start docker compose up -d Stop (keep data) docker compose down Stop + delete all data docker compose down -v View logs docker compose logs -f Restart clean docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d

docs.title: Volume Management
docs.text:
docker volume ls                     # List all volumes
docker volume inspect weaviate_data  # Inspect the Weaviate volume
docker volume rm weaviate_data       # Remove (after docker compose down)

docs.title: Using WeaviateStore in Code
docs.text:
from pattern_language_miner.vector_store.weaviate_store import WeaviateStore

store = WeaviateStore(url="http://localhost:8080", class_name="Pattern")

# Index a pattern
store.upsert_pattern({
    "id": "P-001",
    "name": "Install Package",
    "context": "software setup",
    "solution": "apt-get install <package>",
})

# Semantic search
results = store.query_similar_patterns("how to install software", top_k=5)

# Delete by ID
store.delete_pattern("P-001")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Port 8080 must not be in use by another service.
  • Restart Weaviate if you change your OPENAI_APIKEY.
  • Weaviate uses the text2vec-openai module — each indexing request calls the OpenAI Embeddings API and incurs usage costs.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: All commands are available via the pattern-miner entry-point or python -m pattern_language_miner.cli.

docs.title: Global Options
docs.text:
pattern-miner [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
Option Default Description --log-level INFO Python logging level: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL --help — Show help and exit

docs.title: analyze
docs.text:
Extract frequent lexical n-gram patterns from a directory of documents.

pattern-miner analyze \
  --config <path>       \
  --input-dir <path>    \
  --output-dir <path>
Option Required Description --config Yes Path to the YAML extraction config file --input-dir Yes Directory containing source documents --output-dir Yes Directory where extracted pattern YAMLs are written

Output: One pattern-NNNNN.yaml file per extracted pattern.

Example: 

pattern-miner analyze \
  --config config.yaml \
  --input-dir ./docs \
  --output-dir ./raw-patterns

docs.title: enrich
docs.text:
Add inferred metadata fields (title, summary, problem, keywords) to raw patterns.

pattern-miner enrich \
  --input-dir <path>  \
  --output-dir <path>
Option Required Description --input-dir Yes Directory of raw extracted YAML pattern files --output-dir Yes Directory where enriched files are written (same filenames)

Example: 

pattern-miner enrich \
  --input-dir ./raw-patterns \
  --output-dir ./enriched

docs.title: cluster
docs.text:
Cluster enriched patterns using sentence-transformer embeddings, KMeans, and UMAP.

pattern-miner cluster \
  --input-dir <path>   \
  --output-dir <path>  \
  [--field TEXT]       \
  [--batch-size INT]   \
  [--n-clusters INT]
Option Default Description --input-dir — Directory of enriched YAML pattern files --output-dir — Directory to write clustering output --field solution Pattern field to embed and cluster --batch-size 32 Embedding batch size --n-clusters 5 Number of KMeans clusters

Output:

  • clusters.png — scatter plot of the 2-D UMAP projection
  • clustered_patterns.json — all patterns with a cluster ID field

Example: 

pattern-miner cluster \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-dir ./clusters \
  --n-clusters 8

docs.title: generate-sentences
docs.text:
Convert enriched YAML pattern files into human-readable sentences.

pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir <path>    \
  --output-path <path>  \
  [--format TEXT]
Option Default Description --input-dir — Directory of enriched YAML pattern files --output-path — Destination file for generated output --format text Output format: text, markdown, html

Example: 

pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-path ./patterns.md \
  --format markdown

docs.title: summarize-clusters
docs.text:
Generate a Markdown summary of all clusters from clustered_patterns.json.

pattern-miner summarize-clusters \
  --input-json <path>   \
  --output-path <path>
Option Required Description --input-json Yes Path to the clustered_patterns.json file --output-path Yes Destination Markdown file

Example: 

pattern-miner summarize-clusters \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./cluster-summary.md

docs.title: export-graph
docs.text:
Export enriched pattern data as a knowledge graph in one of four formats.

pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json <path>   \
  --output-path <path>  \
  [--format TEXT]
Option Default Description --input-json — Path to clustered_patterns.json --output-path — Destination file --format graphml Format: graphml, mermaid, neo4j, json

Format details:

Format Use Case graphml Import into Gephi, yEd, or other graph tools mermaid Embed diagrams directly in Markdown files neo4j Import into a Neo4j database via Cypher json Machine-readable node-link JSON

Example: 

pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./graph.mmd \
  --format mermaid

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: The analyze command reads a YAML configuration file that controls every aspect of the extraction process.

docs.title: Minimal Config
docs.text:
pattern_extraction:
  file_type: md
  frequency_threshold: 2
  minimum_token_count: 3
  scope: sentence
  pos_filtering: false
  allowed_pos_tags: []
  block_elements: []
  ngram_min: 2
  ngram_max: 5

docs.title: file_type
docs.text:
Type: string — one of md, txt, html Default: md

The file extension of documents to process. Only files matching this extension inside --input-dir will be read.

file_type: md

docs.title: frequency_threshold
docs.text:
Type: integer ≥ 1 Default: 3

An n-gram must appear at least this many times across all analysed documents to be included in the output. Increase this value to reduce noise; decrease it to capture rarer patterns.

frequency_threshold: 2

docs.title: minimum_token_count
docs.text:
Type: integer ≥ 1 Default: 2

Sentences with fewer tokens than this value are skipped entirely before n-gram extraction. Useful for filtering out headings, labels, and other very short lines.

minimum_token_count: 5

docs.title: scope
docs.text:
Type: string — one of line, sentence, block Default: sentence

Determines how each document is split into analysis units before tokenisation:

Value Behaviour line Each non-empty line is a unit sentence NLTK sentence tokeniser splits the document block Blank-line-separated paragraphs are units 
scope: sentence

docs.title: ngram_min / ngram_max
docs.text:
Type: integer Defaults: ngram_min: 2, ngram_max: 5

The minimum and maximum number of tokens in an extracted n-gram.

ngram_min: 2
ngram_max: 5

docs.title: pos_filtering
docs.text:
Type: boolean Default: false

When true, only sentences in which every token has an allowed POS tag (see allowed_pos_tags) will be processed.

pos_filtering: true

docs.title: allowed_pos_tags
docs.text:
Type: array of strings Default: []

Penn Treebank POS tag codes that are permitted when pos_filtering is enabled. Leave empty to allow all tags.

allowed_pos_tags:
  - NN    # Noun, singular
  - NNS   # Noun, plural
  - VB    # Verb, base form
  - VBZ   # Verb, 3rd person singular present

docs.title: block_elements
docs.text:
Type: array of strings Default: []

When scope is block, include paragraph to split on blank lines.

block_elements:
  - paragraph

docs.title: Full Example Config
docs.text:
pattern_extraction:
  file_type: md
  frequency_threshold: 3
  minimum_token_count: 4
  scope: sentence
  pos_filtering: false
  allowed_pos_tags: []
  block_elements: []
  ngram_min: 2
  ngram_max: 6

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: The configuration is validated at start-up against the JSON Schema in src/pattern_language_miner/schema/config_schema.json. An informative error is raised if a required field is missing or a value is out of range.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This page introduces the core concepts behind Pattern Language Miner and helps you understand what the tool does before you run your first command.

docs.title: What Is a Pattern?
docs.text:
In Pattern Language Miner a pattern is a structured, reusable unit of knowledge with five essential fields:

Field Description context The situation in which the pattern applies problem The need or challenge being addressed solution How the pattern resolves the problem example A concrete illustration sources Files or sections where the pattern was found

This structure is inspired by Christopher Alexander's Pattern Language and Robert E. Horn's Information Mapping.

docs.title: The Pipeline
docs.text:
Pattern Language Miner works as a sequential pipeline:

flowchart TD
    A[Your Documents] --> B(analyze)
    B --> C[Raw Patterns YAML]
    C --> D(enrich)
    D --> E[Enriched Patterns YAML]
    E --> F(cluster)
    E --> G(generate-sentences)
    E --> H(export-graph)
    F --> I[Cluster Report JSON + PNG]
    G --> J[Readable Output md/html/txt]
    H --> K[Knowledge Graph]

Each step is a separate CLI command, so you can run the full pipeline or just the parts you need.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text: Most pipeline behaviour is controlled by a config.yaml file. See Configuration for all available options.

docs.title: YAML Pattern Files
docs.text: Each extracted pattern is saved as an individual YAML file. This makes patterns easy to inspect, version-control, and edit manually.

docs.title: Semantic Clustering
docs.text: The cluster command uses sentence embeddings (via sentence-transformers) to group semantically similar patterns — even if they use different words.

docs.title: Graph Export
docs.text: The export-graph command converts your pattern library into a knowledge graph that can be visualised in tools like Gephi, imported into Neo4j, or embedded in documentation as a Mermaid diagram.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Follow the Quick Start to run your first extraction.
  • Read the Commands reference for all CLI options.
  • Learn about Configuration to tune the extractor.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or later
  • pip
  • (Optional) Docker — for the Weaviate semantic search backend

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/your-org/pattern-language-miner.git
cd pattern-language-miner

# 2. Create a virtual environment
python -m venv ENV
source ENV/bin/activate          # macOS / Linux
# ENV\Scripts\activate           # Windows

# 3. Install the package with all dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"

# 4. Download required NLTK resources
python setup_resources.py

docs.title: Verify the Installation
docs.text:
pattern-miner --help

You should see the top-level help text listing all available commands.

docs.title: Optional: Weaviate (Semantic Search)
docs.text:
If you want to use the vector-search features, start Weaviate via Docker Compose:

docker compose up -d

See Docker & Weaviate for full setup instructions.

docs.title: Upgrade
docs.text: pip install --upgrade -e "."

docs.title: Dependencies Overview
docs.text: Package Purpose click CLI framework nltk Tokenisation and n-gram extraction sentence-transformers Semantic embedding for clustering scikit-learn KMeans clustering umap-learn Dimensionality reduction for visualisation networkx Graph construction and export PyYAML Pattern serialisation beautifulsoup4 HTML parsing weaviate-client Optional vector-store integration

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This walkthrough takes you from a folder of documents to a fully enriched, clustered, and exported pattern library in five commands.

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare Your Documents
docs.text:
Collect the files you want to mine. Supported formats:

  • .txt — plain text
  • .md / .markdown — Markdown
  • .html / .htm — HTML

Place them in a directory, for example ./my-docs/.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Configuration File
docs.text:
Create config.yaml in your working directory:

pattern_extraction:
  file_type: md           # md, txt, or html
  frequency_threshold: 2  # minimum occurrences to count as a pattern
  minimum_token_count: 3  # minimum tokens per sentence to analyse
  scope: sentence         # line | sentence | block
  pos_filtering: false
  allowed_pos_tags: []
  block_elements: []
  ngram_min: 2
  ngram_max: 5

See Configuration for all options and their effects.

docs.title: Step 3: Extract Patterns
docs.text:
pattern-miner analyze \
  --config config.yaml \
  --input-dir ./my-docs \
  --output-dir ./raw-patterns

This produces one YAML file per detected pattern in ./raw-patterns/.

Example output file (raw-patterns/pattern-00001.yaml):

pattern: install the package
frequency: 7

docs.title: Step 4: Enrich Patterns
docs.text:
pattern-miner enrich \
  --input-dir ./raw-patterns \
  --output-dir ./enriched

The enricher adds title, summary, problem, and keywords fields to each pattern.

docs.title: Step 5: Cluster Patterns
docs.text:
pattern-miner cluster \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-dir ./clusters

Outputs: - clusters/clusters.png — scatter-plot of the 2-D UMAP projection - clusters/clustered_patterns.json — patterns with cluster IDs assigned

docs.title: Step 6: Generate Readable Sentences
docs.text:
pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-path ./patterns.md \
  --format markdown

docs.title: Step 7: Export a Knowledge Graph
docs.text:
pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./graph.mmd \
  --format mermaid

The resulting Mermaid diagram can be embedded directly in any Markdown file.

docs.title: Summarise Clusters
docs.text:
pattern-miner summarize-clusters \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./cluster-summary.md

docs.title: Full Pipeline Script
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

pattern-miner analyze    --config config.yaml --input-dir ./docs --output-dir ./raw
pattern-miner enrich     --input-dir ./raw    --output-dir ./enriched
pattern-miner cluster    --input-dir ./enriched --output-dir ./clusters
pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir ./enriched --output-path ./output.md --format markdown
pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./graph.mmd --format mermaid

Logging

Add --log-level DEBUG to any command for verbose output: 

pattern-miner --log-level DEBUG analyze --config config.yaml ...

docs.title: Log Files
docs.text:
Every command writes to logs/pattern_miner.log in your working directory. Use this as your first debugging resource.

# Tail the log in real time
tail -f logs/pattern_miner.log

# View on macOS
open logs/pattern_miner.log

Enable verbose output with:

pattern-miner --log-level DEBUG analyze ...

docs.title: No patterns extracted
docs.text:
Symptom: The output directory is empty after running analyze.

Causes and fixes:

Cause Fix file_type in config doesn't match your files Set file_type: md (or txt, html) to match your documents frequency_threshold too high Lower it to 1 or 2 to capture rarer patterns Documents are very short Lower minimum_token_count to 1 or 2 Wrong --input-dir Verify the path contains files with the expected extension

docs.title: NLTK resource errors
docs.text:
Symptom: 

LookupError: Resource punkt not found.

Fix: 

python setup_resources.py

Or manually inside Python: 

import nltk
nltk.download('punkt')
nltk.download('punkt_tab')
nltk.download('averaged_perceptron_tagger')
nltk.download('averaged_perceptron_tagger_eng')

docs.title: Sentence-transformer model download fails
docs.text:
Symptom: ConnectionError or OSError when running cluster.

Fix: Ensure you have internet access on first run. Models are cached in ~/.cache/huggingface/ after the initial download.

docs.title: Config validation error
docs.text:
Symptom: 

ValidationError: Config validation error: 'frequency_threshold' is a required property

Fix: Compare your config.yaml against the Configuration Reference. All required fields must be present.

docs.title: Weaviate connection refused
docs.text:
Symptom: 

weaviate.exceptions.UnexpectedStatusCodeException: ... refused

Fix: Start Weaviate with Docker Compose: 

docker compose up -d

Then wait ~10 seconds for the service to become healthy before retrying.

docs.title: pattern-miner: command not found
docs.text:
Fix: Activate your virtual environment and ensure the package is installed: 

source ENV/bin/activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"

Or run directly: 

python -m pattern_language_miner.cli --help

docs.title: Cluster scatter plot is empty or unreadable
docs.text:
Symptom: clusters.png shows only one or two points.

Cause: Too few patterns were loaded (UMAP and KMeans require at least as many samples as clusters).

Fix: Lower --n-clusters to match the number of patterns you have, or lower frequency_threshold to extract more patterns.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If none of the above resolves your issue, please open a GitHub issue with:

  1. The exact command you ran
  2. The contents of logs/pattern_miner.log
  3. Your Python version (python --version)
  4. Your OS and package versions (pip freeze)

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text: This page describes common usage workflows, from simple single-step operations to a complete end-to-end pipeline.

docs.title: Workflow 1: Quick Pattern Survey
docs.text:
Use this workflow to get a rapid overview of the recurring language in a corpus.

# Extract patterns with a low threshold
pattern-miner analyze \
  --config config.yaml \
  --input-dir ./docs \
  --output-dir ./raw

# Enrich with readable metadata
pattern-miner enrich \
  --input-dir ./raw \
  --output-dir ./enriched

# Generate a readable Markdown list
pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-path ./overview.md \
  --format markdown

docs.title: Workflow 2: Semantic Clustering & Visualisation
docs.text:
Use this workflow to discover thematic groups within your pattern library.

pattern-miner analyze  --config config.yaml --input-dir ./docs  --output-dir ./raw
pattern-miner enrich   --input-dir ./raw    --output-dir ./enriched

# Cluster with 8 groups, embedding the 'solution' field
pattern-miner cluster \
  --input-dir ./enriched \
  --output-dir ./clusters \
  --field solution \
  --n-clusters 8

# Produce a Markdown summary grouped by cluster
pattern-miner summarize-clusters \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./cluster-report.md

The clusters/clusters.png scatter plot gives a visual overview of how patterns group together.

docs.title: Workflow 3: Knowledge Graph Export
docs.text:
Use this workflow to export your pattern library as a linked knowledge graph.

pattern-miner analyze  --config config.yaml --input-dir ./docs  --output-dir ./raw
pattern-miner enrich   --input-dir ./raw    --output-dir ./enriched
pattern-miner cluster  --input-dir ./enriched --output-dir ./clusters

# Mermaid diagram for documentation
pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./graph.mmd \
  --format mermaid

# Neo4j Cypher for database import
pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json ./clusters/clustered_patterns.json \
  --output-path ./graph.cypher \
  --format neo4j

docs.title: Workflow 4: Full Automation Script
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT="./docs"
CONFIG="./config.yaml"
RAW="./output/raw"
ENRICHED="./output/enriched"
CLUSTERS="./output/clusters"
REPORT="./output/report"

mkdir -p "$RAW" "$ENRICHED" "$CLUSTERS" "$REPORT"

echo "==> Extracting patterns..."
pattern-miner --log-level INFO analyze \
  --config "$CONFIG" \
  --input-dir "$INPUT" \
  --output-dir "$RAW"

echo "==> Enriching patterns..."
pattern-miner enrich \
  --input-dir "$RAW" \
  --output-dir "$ENRICHED"

echo "==> Clustering..."
pattern-miner cluster \
  --input-dir "$ENRICHED" \
  --output-dir "$CLUSTERS" \
  --n-clusters 10

echo "==> Generating sentences..."
pattern-miner generate-sentences \
  --input-dir "$ENRICHED" \
  --output-path "$REPORT/sentences.md" \
  --format markdown

echo "==> Summarising clusters..."
pattern-miner summarize-clusters \
  --input-json "$CLUSTERS/clustered_patterns.json" \
  --output-path "$REPORT/cluster-summary.md"

echo "==> Exporting graph..."
pattern-miner export-graph \
  --input-json "$CLUSTERS/clustered_patterns.json" \
  --output-path "$REPORT/graph.mmd" \
  --format mermaid

echo "Done! Results in $REPORT"

docs.title: Workflow 5: Incremental Updates
docs.text:
If your document corpus grows, you can re-run only the steps that need updating:

# Only re-extract from new documents (add them to ./docs)
pattern-miner analyze --config config.yaml --input-dir ./docs --output-dir ./raw

# Re-enrich and re-cluster
pattern-miner enrich  --input-dir ./raw     --output-dir ./enriched
pattern-miner cluster --input-dir ./enriched --output-dir ./clusters

Note

Each run overwrites the output directory. Archive previous results before re-running if you need to compare outputs.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use --log-level DEBUG on any command for verbose diagnostic output.
  • All output directories are created automatically — no need to mkdir first.
  • Tune frequency_threshold and ngram_min/ngram_max in config.yaml if the number of extracted patterns is too high or too low.
